title: The role of oxytocin in rebuilding trust after betrayal
author: Kalesh
excerpt: You know that feeling when someone you trusted completely breaks that trust. The ground shifts. Your body knows before your mind does. Something in your chest...
metaDescription: You know that feeling when someone you trusted completely breaks that trust. The ground shifts. Your body knows before your mind does. Something in your c...
htmlContent:
You know that feeling when someone you trusted completely breaks that trust. The ground shifts. Your body knows before your mind does. Something in your chest tightens, your stomach drops, and suddenly the world feels like a less safe place. I've been there. You've been there. It's a specific kind of pain that doesn't just hurt your feelings. It rewires your nervous system.

Here's the thing. We don't heal from betrayal by thinking our way out of it. We heal through the body. And there's a molecule that plays a bigger role in this than most people realize. Oxytocin. You've probably heard it called the "love hormone" or the "bonding chemical." But that's like calling a Ferrari a "car." It's technically true, but it misses everything that matters.

Let's talk about what oxytocin actually does when you've been betrayed. And more , how you can work with it to rebuild trust without forcing yourself into something your body isn't ready for.

What oxytocin is actually doing in your body

Oxytocin is a neuropeptide. It's produced in the hypothalamus and released into your brain and bloodstream. Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed Polyvagal Theory, has written extensively about how oxytocin interacts with the vagus nerve and the social engagement system. His work shows that oxytocin helps quiet the defensive circuits in your nervous system. It makes social connection feel safe again.

But here's where it gets complicated. After betrayal, your body learns that connection is dangerous. Your nervous system associates closeness with potential harm. So when someone gets too close, your body releases cortisol and adrenaline instead of oxytocin. You go into protection mode. That's not a character flaw. That's your survival brain doing exactly what it evolved to do.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma lives in the body as incomplete defensive responses. When you were betrayed, your body probably wanted to fight, flee, or freeze. If you couldn't complete those responses, the energy got stuck. And now your nervous system is hypervigilant, scanning for signs of danger even when you're with people who haven't hurt you.

Oxytocin is the chemical that can help your nervous system shift out of that protective state. But you can't just will it to happen. You can't force yourself to trust. Your body won't let you. And honestly, it shouldn't. Blind trust after betrayal isn't healing. It's denial.

Sit with that for a moment. Real rebuilding starts when you respect your body's warning system instead of overriding it.

How betrayal disrupts your oxytocin system

When you experience betrayal, especially from someone you were bonded with, your oxytocin receptors can actually downregulate. That means your cells become less sensitive to oxytocin. Your body is saying, "We tried that closeness thing and it hurt. Let's not do that again."

This is why you might feel numb, disconnected, or unable to feel warmth toward people you used to love. It's not that you don't care. It's that your body has turned down the volume on bonding signals to protect you. Janina Fisher, a trauma specialist who worked closely with Bessel van der Kolk, describes this as a survival strategy. Your system is trying to prevent future injury by reducing your capacity for attachment.

But here's the problem. We are wired for connection. We need it. Complete withdrawal from bonding is not sustainable. Your nervous system knows this too. So you end up in this awful tug of war. Part of you craves closeness. Another part of you is terrified of it. And neither part is wrong.

Wild, right? Your body is simultaneously trying to protect you from the thing you most need.

This is why so many people get stuck in cycles after betrayal. They try to force trust too quickly, hit a wall of anxiety, and then retreat even further. Or they give up on connection entirely and isolate. Neither approach works because neither approach addresses the underlying nervous system state.

One supplement that many people find calming for their nervous system is Doctors Best Magnesium Glycinate. Magnesium plays a role in regulating the HPA axis and supporting GABA function, which can help your nervous system settle enough to even begin this work. It's not a cure. But it can make the foundation more stable.

Small ways to rebuild oxytocin signaling

So how do you actually work with your oxytocin system after betrayal? The answer is not dramatic. It's not about grand gestures or forced intimacy. It's about tiny, repeated experiences of safety that your body can slowly learn to trust.

Start with your own body. Oxytocin is released through gentle touch, warm temperature, slow breathing, and even certain sounds. You don't need another person to access these pathways. You can begin alone.

Try placing your hand on your own chest or belly. Breathe slowly. Let your exhale be longer than your inhale. This activates the vagus nerve and can trigger oxytocin release. It feels silly at first. I know. But your nervous system doesn't care about dignity. It cares about safety signals.

Warm baths, soft blankets, gentle rocking. These all stimulate oxytocin. You're not being indulgent. You're literally rebuilding the neural pathways that allow you to bond with others. Think about it. You can't give someone trust you don't have for yourself.

When you do start connecting with others, keep it small. Five minutes of eye contact with a safe person. A hug that lasts longer than twenty seconds (that's the threshold for meaningful oxytocin release). Sitting side by side without talking. These micro-moments of connection are where trust gets rebuilt, one tiny repair at a time.

Deb Dana, who specializes in applying Polyvagal Theory clinically, calls these "glimmers." Small moments that signal safety to your nervous system. They're the opposite of triggers. And they're essential for rewiring your capacity for trust.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk. It explains the science of how trauma affects the body and brain in ways that are deeply validating. Reading it can help you understand why your nervous system is responding the way it is.

What safe connection actually looks like

Here's the hard truth. You cannot rebuild trust with someone who isn't safe. Your oxytocin system will not cooperate with a person who continues to trigger your threat responses. And it shouldn't. That's not a failure of healing. That's your body being honest with you.

Real safety looks like consistency over time. It looks like someone who respects your boundaries even when you're not sure what they are. It looks like someone who can handle your "no" without punishing you for it. It looks like repair after rupture, not just pretending the rupture didn't happen.

If you're trying to rebuild trust with someone who betrayed you, pay attention to how your body feels around them. Not what your mind thinks. What your body feels. Do you feel a sense of expansion or contraction? Do you feel more alive or more drained? Your nervous system is giving you information every second. The question is whether you're willing to listen.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice by Peter Levine. It goes deep into how the body processes trauma and how you can work with somatic experiences to complete unfinished protective responses. It's not light reading. But it's real.

You might also find value in Anchored by Deb Dana. It's a practical guide to working with your nervous system, with exercises you can actually do. It's grounded in Polyvagal Theory but written for regular people who are just trying to feel better.

The timeline is not linear

I want to be honest with you about something. Rebuilding trust after betrayal is not a straight line. You will have days where you feel connected and hopeful. And then something small will happen and you'll be right back in the fear. That's not regression. That's how the nervous system works.

Your oxytocin system doesn't operate on a schedule. It responds to context. If you're stressed, tired, hungry, or in a triggering environment, your capacity for trust will shrink. That's normal. That's human.

The goal is not to reach some permanent state of trust where nothing ever bothers you again. The goal is to build enough capacity that you can move through the inevitable moments of doubt without abandoning yourself or the relationship. The goal is to let your body know, through repeated experience, that connection can be safe even after it wasn't safe before.

Notice what just happened in your body when you read that. Did something relax? Did something tighten? That's your nervous system talking. Keep listening.

I don't have a neat conclusion for you. I don't think there is one. Betrayal changes you. It changes how you see the world and how you move through it. But here's what I've learned from my own experience and from the science. Your oxytocin system is not broken. It's just been protecting you. And with time, with patience, with small repeated experiences of safety, it can learn to open again.

Not because you've forgotten what happened. But because your body has finally understood that not everyone is the person who hurt you.
